title: Génoise sponge
subtitle: French whole-egg sponge base for layered and filled cakes
excerpt: A light, fine-crumbed sponge built by whisking whole eggs and sugar together over heat until pale and thick, then folding in sifted flour and melted butter. It has no chemical leavening: all the lift comes from the aerated eggs. The texture is firmer and denser than a Victoria sponge, which makes it well-suited to slicing into layers, brushing with syrup, and filling with cream, fruit, or buttercream.
sourceNotes: Génoise originates in Genoa and became a staple of French and Italian patisserie in the nineteenth century. Auguste Escoffier, Le Guide Culinaire (1903, first English translation 1907, Project Gutenberg), describes génoise as a foundational element of French pastry work, distinguished from the beaten-butter Victoria by its whole-egg whipping method. Jules Gouffé, Le Livre de Cuisine (1867, Project Gutenberg), gives a classic recipe consistent with the warm-whisking method still used today. The technique is unchanged in professional patisserie since.

content.text: Génoise has no chemical leavening: all the lift comes from air beaten into the eggs. The mix must reach the

before the flour goes in. Once the flour and melted butter are added, fold with a large metal spoon using as few strokes as possible; over-mixing deflates the foam and produces a dense, rubbery sponge.

Heat the oven to 180°C conventional (160°C fan). Grease a 20cm round tin and line the base with baking paper. Set a heatproof bowl over a pan of barely simmering water, making sure the base does not touch the water. Add the eggs, caster sugar, and vanilla to the bowl and whisk continuously until the mixture is warm to the touch and leaves a thick, slowly dissolving ribbon from the beater when lifted. This takes 5 to 8 minutes with a hand whisk or 4 to 5 minutes with a stand mixer.

Remove from the heat and continue whisking (in a stand mixer if possible) until the mixture has cooled, tripled in volume, and is pale and very thick; about 5 more minutes. Pour the cooled melted butter around the inside edge of the bowl. Sift one-third of the flour over the top. Fold very gently with a large metal spoon, using broad scooping strokes from the bottom. Add the remaining flour in two batches and fold until just incorporated; a few faint flour streaks are preferable to over-mixing. Pour carefully into the prepared tin.

Bake for 22 to 26 minutes without opening the oven door until the cake is golden, risen slightly above the rim, and a skewer comes out clean. The surface should spring back when pressed lightly. Leave in the tin for 5 minutes, then turn onto a rack. Remove the baking paper and leave to cool completely. Once cold, slice into layers and soak with flavoured syrup before filling.
